learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, HashSet, Loops, Time Complexity, Basic Searching
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine attendance IDs arriving in random order. You want to find the longest streak of consecutive IDs without sorting the entire list.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine attendance IDs arriving in random order. You want to find the longest streak of consecutive IDs without sorting the entire list.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Hash Set pattern whenever you need fast O(1) lookups to detect consecutive sequences or neighboring values without sorting.
learning.signals: Whenever the problem asks for consecutive elements in an unsorted array with O(n) time, think of using a HashSet.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an unsorted array of integers nums, return the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time.

examples.explanation: The longest consecutive sequence is [1,2,3,4], whose length is 4.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: For every number, repeatedly search for the next consecutive number in the array.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Store all numbers in a HashSet. A number starts a sequence only if its previous number (num - 1) does not exist. From every starting number, count consecutive values until the sequence ends.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Only numbers without a predecessor can start a new consecutive sequence.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever the problem asks for consecutive elements in an unsorted array with O(n) time, think of using a HashSet.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each number is processed at most once while expanding sequences. HashSet lookups are O(1) on average, giving an overall O(n) solution.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever the problem asks for consecutive elements in an unsorted array with O(n) time, think of using a HashSet.
commonWrongPaths: Starting from every element instead of sequence starts.
Using a list instead of a HashSet.

hints: Avoid sorting because O(n log n) is not optimal.
Use a HashSet for O(1) lookup.
Start counting only from sequence beginnings.
commonMisconceptions: Starting from every element instead of sequence starts.
Using a list instead of a HashSet.

pattern.name: Hash Set
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever the problem asks for consecutive elements in an unsorted array with O(n) time, think of using a HashSet.

approaches.title: Check Every Sequence
approaches.idea: For every number, repeatedly search for the next consecutive number in the array.
algorithm: Search for the next consecutive value.
approaches.dryRun: Repeatedly scan the array for consecutive numbers.

code.java: Nested search solution omitted because of quadratic complexity.

approaches.title: Sorting
approaches.idea: Sort the array and count consecutive numbers while ignoring duplicates.

approaches.title: Hash Set
approaches.idea: Insert every element into a HashSet. Only begin expanding a sequence if the previous number does not exist. Continue checking num+1, num+2, ... until the sequence ends. Keep track of the maximum sequence length.
approaches.intuition: Each number is processed at most once while expanding sequences. HashSet lookups are O(1) on average, giving an overall O(n) solution.
algorithm: Insert all numbers into a HashSet.
If num-1 exists, skip because it is not a sequence start.
Otherwise count consecutive numbers using the HashSet.

visualization.description: Only start counting from the beginning of each sequence.
steps: Store all numbers in a HashSet.
Expand forward while consecutive numbers exist.

interviewFollowUps: Why do we check only sequence starts?
What changes if duplicates are removed?
interviewTips: Show why each element is visited once.
State O(n) time and O(n) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Only numbers without a predecessor can start a new consecutive sequence.
cheatSheet.algorithm: HashSet → Find Starts → Expand → Update Maximum.

commonMistakes: Starting from every element instead of sequence starts.
Using a list instead of a HashSet.
